research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.

baseline_question_bank.research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.
baseline_question_bank.personalized_opening: I'd love to understand how Pulse fits into your day-to-day.

questions.primary: What makes you reach for another tool instead of Pulse?
questions.rationale_gap: Broad opener.

questions.primary: Walk me through the last time you tried to get an answer from Pulse.
questions.rationale_gap: Broad behavioral prompt.

questions.primary: What would make Pulse part of your daily workflow?
questions.rationale_gap: Broad aspiration prompt.

final_insight_doc.research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.

themes.label: Query builder too complex for power users and non-technical users alike — but for opposite reasons

findings.text: Non-technical users find the query builder overwhelming for simple tasks, while technical users hit a ceiling when they need advanced SQL constructs, higher query limits, or API access — the tool satisfies neither end of the spectrum.

supporting_quotes.quote: it's too complex to get a simple pipeline number out of it. I couldn't figure out the query builder at all, all these joins and filters and dimension dropdowns. I just need one number.
The builder is fine for simple cuts, but the second I want a window function or a weird join, I hit a wall. I'm technical, I'd rather just write the query than fight the dropdowns.
I don't want a UI, I want an API and raw query access so I can build what I need. Every time the roadmap is about prettier charts, I think, you're solving the wrong problem for me.
Everyone assumes power users churn because it's too complicated. For me it's the opposite, I churn because it's too constrained. Give me more room, not guardrails.

It drops you into this query builder thing, and the query builder is intimidating. I open it and have no idea where to start. There are all these dropdowns and fields and operators, and I just sit there thinking, what am I supposed to put here?

That's the opposite of what I need. A builder means more work for me. I'm not going to sit there assembling charts. Make it simpler, not more powerful. Give me the one screen and I'm a happy customer.

themes.label: Spreadsheets, source tools, and custom-built solutions become the real source of truth

findings.text: Users across Sales Ops, Support, Marketing, Finance, Engineering, Customer Success, Product Analysis, and Operations bypass Pulse entirely — exporting to spreadsheets, staying in source systems, building their own dashboards, pulling data manually, waiting on the data team, or escaping to notebooks — which become the team's de facto source of truth.

supporting_quotes.quote: My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse.
We live in Zendesk. I've got a dashboard there for ticket volume, first response time, backlog by queue. That's what we look at in standup every morning. Pulse is kind of off to the side for us.
I export the campaign table, drop in our GA and spend numbers next to it, and reconcile by hand. It's tedious, but at least I know exactly where every figure came from.
the moment something has to be official, I go back to the general ledger. Everything I produce has to reconcile to the penny, and Pulse can't give me that.
About two months ago we gave up and built our own Grafana dashboard off the raw tables. It does exactly what we need and nobody on my team has to fight the tool to get a number out.
I end up dumping raw data into my own notebook and doing the real work in pandas, which kind of defeats the whole point of having Pulse.

I pinged someone on the analytics team and asked them to pull it for me. I needed shipment turnaround times for last month, and rather than fight with Pulse I just waited two days for them to send me a spreadsheet.

I went in, picked the wrong dataset apparently, got a number that looked off, second-guessed it, then just Slacked an analyst and asked her to send me the real figure. That's what I do every time, honestly.

themes.label: Slow query performance and timeouts undermine trust and usability

findings.text: Full-scale queries take so long to load — or time out entirely — that users abandon Pulse mid-workflow; the lag and timeout ceiling scale with data volume and query complexity, meaning the heaviest users suffer most.

supporting_quotes.quote: I've literally walked to get coffee and come back and it's still loading.

A toy dataset is fine, but the moment you point it at a quarter of production events, you're staring at a spinner for fifteen, twenty seconds. My team just stopped opening it because waiting felt worse than writing the query ourselves.
The lag scales with how much you actually ask of it, so the people who needed it most got the worst experience.
Any complex query I write just times out. The second I join more than a couple of tables or look at a wide date range, it spins and then dies.

findings.text: The manual export-plus-pivot or notebook workflow is faster end-to-end than waiting for a single Pulse query to complete, making workarounds the rational choice.

supporting_quotes.quote: The export plus pivot is faster than waiting on one Pulse query to load.
If I had an endpoint I trusted and it didn't lag when I loaded anything real, I'd kill our Grafana dashboard tomorrow.
Raise the query timeout ceiling and let me opt into bigger limits... Do that and I'd move my whole workflow into Pulse tomorrow instead of treating it as a glorified export button.
it's faster to ask a person. That's the whole story.

themes.label: Data accuracy mismatches and known bugs destroy trust in Pulse numbers

findings.text: When Pulse figures disagree with source-system numbers, users lose confidence in all Pulse data, stop presenting it to stakeholders, and rebuild figures manually to defend in meetings.

supporting_quotes.quote: Pulse will say we closed 340 tickets yesterday, Zendesk says 312. I have no idea which is right, and if I can't trust the number I'm not going to put it in front of my VP.
The first time the number is wrong, people stop believing all of them. My team already rolls their eyes when I pull up Pulse.
Someone in finance cross-checked revenue against the source system and it didn't match. Once that happens once, they question every number.
Last month it showed our paid sessions about 18% lower than GA for the same week. When I can't reconcile it, I just stop believing the dashboard.

Last quarter a revenue number in Pulse was off from the GL by about forty thousand dollars. I can't put a figure in front of the CFO that doesn't tie out. One mismatch and I lose all trust in the whole tool.
If I'm sitting in front of a customer presenting their own health score, I can't have the number be three days old or have just gotten unblocked that morning.
I went in, picked the wrong dataset apparently, got a number that looked off, second-guessed it, then just Slacked an analyst and asked her to send me the real figure.

findings.text: A confirmed join bug causes double-counting in at least one data model; this is a root cause of discrepancies users are noticing and the highest-priority fix according to the data engineer.

supporting_quotes.quote: There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed.
Fix the join bug first. Freshness and access are friction, but the double-count is the thing that quietly kills trust.

findings.text: Users suspect discrepancies stem from definitional differences but feel they shouldn't have to investigate — Pulse offers no metric-definition transparency or data lineage, and the burden of proof falls on the tool. Non-technical users additionally can't identify which metric to pick in the first place because terminology is opaque.

supporting_quotes.quote: I think Pulse counts merged tickets differently, or maybe it's a timezone thing on the day boundary. But that's the point, right? I shouldn't have to play detective.
if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.

I think it pulls from a different cut of the data with its own logic on refunds and intercompany entries. But because I can't trace a number in Pulse back to a journal entry, it's a black box, and a black box doesn't reconcile.
Something will say 'DAU cohort delta' and I'm like, is that the thing I want? I genuinely can't tell, so I don't trust whatever I pick.

themes.label: Stale data makes Pulse unusable for operational and time-critical decisions

findings.text: Data is stale by design (6-hour sync cycle, multi-day lags during close, or days-old account health signals), making Pulse structurally unfit for intraday, event-driven, customer-facing, or month-end decisions across multiple teams.

supporting_quotes.quote: The ticket data in Pulse is hours stale, it's not real-time. By the time it caught up the fire was already out.
The sync into Pulse only runs every six hours, so the data is stale by design. When the growth team opens a dashboard at 9am, they're looking at numbers from 3am.
During close it can lag by two or three days. There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at.
A churn signal that's three days old isn't a signal, it's a postmortem. I need to act the morning a usage drop happens, not at the end of the week when the data finally rolls up.
Last month a mid-market account churned and Pulse still showed them green three days after they'd told us they were leaving.

findings.text: Multiple users across teams would actively switch to Pulse if trust, freshness, and access issues were resolved — indicating latent demand, not rejection of the product concept.

supporting_quotes.quote: If it's real-time and the counts match, I'd happily switch because Pulse actually slices trends across teams better than Zendesk does.
If I could write raw SQL and the totals actually matched GA and our ad spend, I'd have no reason to rebuild everything in a Google Sheet.
Make it reconcile to the general ledger, to the dollar, with a clear lineage back to journal entries, and refresh it fast enough to keep up with the close.
Fix the freshness and the access first, those are the dealbreakers.
If I had an endpoint I trusted and it didn't lag when I loaded anything real, I'd kill our Grafana dashboard tomorrow.
I'd actually use it if it gave me more query power and bigger limits, not fewer. Give me more room, not guardrails. Do that and I'd move my whole workflow into Pulse tomorrow.

One simple dashboard with the three or four numbers my team cares about, in plain language, that I can open and immediately understand. No building, no guessing what a metric means. Just glance, get my answer, and get back to work.
If I log in and my region's numbers are just there, simple, accurate, no hunting, I'd use it every day.

themes.label: Broken access permissions — both at onboarding and for cross-team analysis — cause users to abandon Pulse before first value

findings.text: New analysts encounter greyed-out datasets on joining, CSMs can't access newly-assigned account data until a ticket clears, and power analysts are walled off from cross-team data they need for multi-team analyses — multi-day permission delays kill momentum and route work back to the data team.

supporting_quotes.quote: A new analyst joins, opens Pulse, and half the datasets are greyed out. By the time their permissions clear, they've already given up and asked me to just pull the data for them.
I'm flooded with access-request tickets from people who can't see the tables they need.

I have to file a request with the data team just to see account-level data, and it takes days. So for a brand-new account I'm assigned, I can't even pull their numbers until a ticket clears. That kills any momentum in the first week.

I keep hitting permission walls, I can't see other teams' data even when I need it for a cross-team analysis. So I filed an access request, waited two days, got half of it approved. The analysis was already stale by then.

Almost every interesting question I have is cross-team, and that's exactly the data I'm walled off from. The permissions are scoped per team, so the moment I reach across a boundary I'm stuck filing tickets and pinging admins. It kills the momentum every time.

themes.label: Absence of data lineage, freshness indicators, and plain-language metric definitions forces users to distrust Pulse by default

findings.text: Users cannot see when data was last refreshed or trace a Pulse number back to its source calculation; non-technical users additionally cannot decode jargon metric names to know if they've selected the right metric — forcing everyone to assume the worst and revert to source systems for anything consequential.

supporting_quotes.quote: There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at.
because I can't trace a number in Pulse back to a journal entry, it's a black box, and a black box doesn't reconcile.
With Pulse, if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.
I think Pulse counts merged tickets differently, or maybe it's a timezone thing on the day boundary. But that's the point, right? I shouldn't have to play detective.
Fix the freshness and the access first, those are the dealbreakers.
Half of them I have to guess at. Something will say 'DAU cohort delta' and I'm like, is that the thing I want? I genuinely can't tell, so I don't trust whatever I pick.

open_questions: The join bug is the confirmed root cause of at least some discrepancies — which specific data models are affected, and does the finance revenue model (source of Grace's $40K GL mismatch) contain the same bug as the marketing campaign table?
Is there a roadmap or timeline to fix the join bug, and do product/engineering stakeholders understand it as the top trust-blocker across Finance, Marketing, and Support?

Tomás, Hannah, and Raj all want raw SQL / higher query limits / API access — how many other technical users are hitting the query-builder ceiling or timeout wall, and is a SQL escape-hatch, configurable timeout ceiling, or API endpoint already on the product roadmap?

Hannah's team built a Grafana dashboard off raw tables and Raj escapes to pandas notebooks — how many other engineering or technical teams have similarly routed around Pulse entirely? Is this shadow-infrastructure pattern tracked anywhere?

Hannah cited performance scaling with query size and Raj reports outright timeouts on multi-table joins — is the performance bottleneck in the query engine layer, the data warehouse, or the Pulse application layer? Has engineering investigated this?

Would exposing metric definitions, calculation logic, and data lineage inline (e.g., hover-over tooltips, a plain-language data dictionary, or journal-entry traceability for Finance) reduce the 'I can't defend this number' and 'I can't decode this metric name' barriers enough to keep both technical and non-technical users in Pulse?

Raj confirmed that cross-team permission walls are a weekly blocker for analysts doing cross-functional work — is the per-team permission scoping a deliberate data-governance decision, or a legacy artifact that could be redesigned with row-level security or data-sharing agreements?

What would it take to reduce the sync cadence from 6 hours to near-real-time — and separately, could a data-freshness timestamp be added immediately as a low-effort trust signal even before sync speed improves?
Grace's month-end lag is 2–3 days and Owen's account health data is days behind — are these the same infrastructure constraint as the 6-hour sync, or separate pipeline delays?

Owen mentioned wanting a proactive per-customer morning digest via email rather than having to log in — is there latent demand for push/alert-style delivery among other operational users (CSMs, Support leads, Sales Directors, Operations Coordinators) who need signals without actively querying?
The latent demand signal is now confirmed by eight interviewees (Marcus, Tomás, Grace, Owen, Hannah, Raj, Lucia, Derek) — is this reflected anywhere in product prioritization discussions?

Dana, Lucia, Derek, Marcus, Owen, and Grace all describe wanting pre-built role-specific dashboards — is there a product concept for curated 'starter dashboards' per team/role, and would this require a metric-naming taxonomy overhaul? How many non-technical users represent the Sales Director / field-leadership persona Derek embodies?

Derek explicitly called out being on the road and wanting a mobile-accessible view — is there a mobile or lightweight view on the roadmap, and how many field-facing personas (Sales Directors, CSMs) share this constraint?
Is there a configurable query timeout or tiered query-limit system on the roadmap that would let power users like Raj and Hannah opt into heavier compute at the cost of longer wait times?

contradictions.description: There is a genuine tension between what different user types need from Pulse's query interface: non-technical users (Dana Whitfield, Marcus Lee, Grace Kim, Owen Bradley, Lucia Romano, Derek Olsen) want simpler pre-built dashboards, plain-language metric names, clear reconciled outputs, and less query-building complexity, while technical users (Tomás Herrera, Hannah Ortiz, Raj Patel, and implicitly Priya Raman) want more power — raw SQL access, higher query timeout limits, an API endpoint, window functions, and fewer guardrails. These are mutually exclusive interface philosophies; optimizing for one risks alienating the other. Hannah's team abandoned Pulse entirely for a custom Grafana dashboard, and Raj escapes to pandas notebooks, rather than work within the UI constraints. Derek explicitly said the new report builder is 'the opposite of what I need.'

final_question_bank.research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.
final_question_bank.personalized_opening: We've heard from Sales, Finance, Engineering, and Support that Pulse isn't fitting into their daily workflows — I'd love to understand specifically how you use data day-to-day and where Pulse does or doesn't show up.

questions.primary: Derek Olsen told us he wants exactly three numbers — bookings, pipeline, attainment — visible the moment he logs in, with no filters or building required. Does that description match what you or others on your team need from a daily data touchpoint?
questions.rationale_gap: Six non-technical users (Dana, Marcus, Grace, Owen, Lucia, Derek) have now independently described wanting pre-built role-specific views. We need to confirm whether this is a universal non-technical user pattern or specific to certain roles, and whether specific metric sets per role are consistent enough to build toward.
probes: If you could define the three to five numbers that should greet you on login, what would they be?
Would a pre-built view actually replace your current workaround, or would you still need to go elsewhere for certain data?
Have you ever seen a tool that got this right? What made it work?
grounding: Derek Olsen: 'I just want one simple summary screen of my region. Bookings, pipeline, attainment, the three things I actually care about, right there when I log in. No building, no filters. Just show me.'
Lucia Romano: 'One simple dashboard with the three or four numbers my team cares about, in plain language, that I can open and immediately understand.'

questions.primary: Derek told us his standard fallback is to Slack an analyst when Pulse fails him — and Priya Raman said she's flooded with exactly those requests. How often does your team route data requests through an analyst instead of self-serving in Pulse, and what triggers that decision?
questions.rationale_gap: Both the demand side (Derek, Lucia, Dana) and the supply side (Priya) confirm analyst-as-middleman is a systemic pattern. We need to quantify how widespread this is and understand whether this is seen as a cost or accepted as normal.
probes: Is pinging an analyst faster, or is it about trust in the number they'll return?
Has anyone on your team tried Pulse and given up, settling permanently on asking an analyst?
Do you think this analyst-mediated workflow is sustainable, or does it create bottlenecks?
grounding: Derek Olsen: 'I went in, picked the wrong dataset apparently, got a number that looked off, second-guessed it, then just Slacked an analyst and asked her to send me the real figure. That's what I do every time, honestly.'
Priya Raman: 'I'm flooded with access-request tickets from people who can't see the tables they need.'

questions.primary: Multiple people have told us they picked a dataset in Pulse, got a number that looked wrong, and immediately lost confidence — Grace Kim described a $40K revenue discrepancy, Marcus Lee saw ticket counts that didn't match Zendesk. Has that happened to you, and how did it change how much you rely on Pulse afterward?
questions.rationale_gap: Data trust is a confirmed high-confidence theme. We want to probe whether the trust collapse is universal once a discrepancy is seen, or whether some users find ways to verify and recover trust. Derek's quote about second-guessing a number fits this pattern for a Sales Director persona we haven't fully explored.
probes: After a wrong number, did you stop using Pulse entirely, or just for that data type?
Did you ever get an explanation for why the number was off, or did it just stay a mystery?
Would seeing a clear definition of how each metric is calculated change whether you'd trust it?
grounding: Derek Olsen: 'I went in, picked the wrong dataset apparently, got a number that looked off, second-guessed it.'
Marcus Lee: 'The first time the number is wrong, people stop believing all of them.'
Grace Kim: 'Last quarter a revenue number in Pulse was off from the GL by about forty thousand dollars.'

questions.primary: Derek said he's on the road constantly and is never at his desk when he needs a number. Owen Bradley similarly needs real-time signals from wherever he is. How are field-facing roles like yours currently getting data when you're not at a desktop — and would a lightweight or mobile-accessible Pulse view actually change your behavior?
questions.rationale_gap: Derek introduced a mobile/field-access dimension not previously raised. We need to test whether this is a widespread constraint for Sales Directors, CSMs, and other field roles — and whether it represents a distinct adoption barrier or is subsumed by the pre-built dashboard need.
probes: What device do you most often have available when you need a quick number before a meeting?
If Pulse had a mobile-friendly summary view, would you open it instead of pinging an analyst?
Is the lack of mobile access a blocker in itself, or is it secondary to the complexity and trust issues?
grounding: Derek Olsen: 'it'd be great as a mobile app, by the way, I'm on the road constantly and I'm never at my desk when I need a number.'
Owen Bradley: 'Fix the freshness and the access first, those are the dealbreakers.'

questions.primary: Derek described needing his West region bookings for a QBR and not trusting what Pulse returned — ending up using analyst-provided data instead. How do you currently prepare data for leadership-facing meetings like QBRs, and what would it take for Pulse to be the source you'd actually cite in that room?
questions.rationale_gap: QBR and leadership-presentation scenarios are high-stakes moments where trust failures are most costly. Several interviewees (Tomás, Grace, Marcus) mentioned avoiding Pulse for anything going to leadership. The Sales Director QBR context is a new high-stakes scenario worth probing to understand the 'presentable data' standard.
probes: What's your bar for a number being 'presentable' to leadership versus just directionally useful?
If Pulse showed a clear data lineage or last-refreshed timestamp next to a number, would that raise your confidence enough to cite it?
Who else in the room at a QBR is pulling data from Pulse versus other sources — and do they ever conflict?
grounding: Derek Olsen: 'Last Monday I needed my West region bookings for the QBR. I went in, picked the wrong dataset apparently, got a number that looked off, second-guessed it, then just Slacked an analyst.'
Tomás Herrera: 'if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.'

steps.transcript_summary: Dana, a Sales Ops Lead, avoids Pulse because its query builder is too complex for simple pipeline numbers and it's painfully slow on real queries, so she defaults to exporting raw data into a shared spreadsheet.

themes.label: Query builder too complex for simple tasks

findings.text: Non-technical users find the query builder (joins, filters, dimension dropdowns) overwhelming when they only need a single simple metric.

supporting_quotes.quote: it's too complex to get a simple pipeline number out of it. I couldn't figure out the query builder at all, all these joins and filters and dimension dropdowns. I just need one number.

themes.label: Spreadsheets have become the real source of truth

findings.text: Users bypass Pulse entirely by exporting raw data to spreadsheets, which become the team's de facto source of truth.

supporting_quotes.quote: My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse.

themes.label: Slow query performance undermines trust and usability

findings.text: Full-scale queries take so long to load that users abandon Pulse mid-workflow, finding the wait incompatible with time-pressured work.

supporting_quotes.quote: I've literally walked to get coffee and come back and it's still loading.

findings.text: The manual export-plus-pivot workflow is faster end-to-end than waiting for a single Pulse query to complete.

supporting_quotes.quote: The export plus pivot is faster than waiting on one Pulse query to load.

themes.label: Pulse doesn't fit into fast-paced, time-pressured workflows

open_questions: Do other non-sales teams (e.g., marketing, finance, customer success) face the same query-builder complexity barrier, or is this Sales Ops-specific?
Is the performance problem widespread across all query types and user roles, or concentrated in large cross-rep, multi-quarter queries?
Are teams aware that their shared spreadsheets may be out of sync with Pulse data, and does data trust factor into the workaround decision?
Do any users actually succeed with the query builder — and if so, what differentiates them (technical background, training, use case)?
Would pre-built, one-click dashboards for common metrics (e.g., committed pipeline by region) resolve the adoption barrier for non-technical users?

question_bank.research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.
question_bank.personalized_opening: We've heard from one user that getting even a single simple metric out of Pulse feels harder than just exporting to a spreadsheet — we'd love to understand whether that resonates with your experience or looks different from your vantage point.

questions.primary: Can you walk me through the last time you used Pulse — what were you trying to find out, and what actually happened?
questions.rationale_gap: We have only one data point (Dana) on the end-to-end Pulse experience. We need to know whether the complexity and slowness pattern repeats for a second user in a potentially different role.
probes: What made you go to Pulse rather than another source?
At what point, if any, did you hit a wall or give up?
What did you do to get the answer you needed?
grounding: Dana Whitfield: 'I opened it maybe twice last quarter... it just doesn't fit how I work day to day.'

questions.primary: Outside of Pulse, where does your team actually go to get the numbers it relies on day-to-day?
questions.rationale_gap: Dana's team built a shared spreadsheet as a replacement source of truth. We need to know if this is a widespread pattern — other teams operating on stale exports — or specific to Sales Ops.
probes: How often is that source updated, and who maintains it?
Has anyone ever questioned whether those numbers match what's in Pulse or the underlying data systems?
What would it take for Pulse to replace that source?
grounding: Dana Whitfield: 'My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse.'

questions.primary: Have you ever tried to build a custom query or filter in Pulse — and if so, what was that experience like?
questions.rationale_gap: We need to confirm whether the query builder is a broad adoption barrier or specific to Dana's non-technical profile, and whether more technically fluent users succeed with it.
probes: What were you trying to get to? How far did you get?
What specifically stopped you or slowed you down — was it the interface, unclear terminology, or something else?
Did you ever get help from a colleague or find documentation useful?
grounding: Dana Whitfield: 'I couldn't figure out the query builder at all, all these joins and filters and dimension dropdowns. I just need one number.'

questions.primary: When you run queries in Pulse, how would you describe the speed — and has that ever changed what you decided to do?
questions.rationale_gap: Dana explicitly cited loading time as a deal-breaker. We need a second data point to assess whether this is a widespread dealbreaker or specific to large Sales Ops queries.
probes: Can you remember a specific time when you were waiting and decided to go elsewhere?
Do smaller or simpler queries feel faster to you?
If Pulse loaded instantly, would that change how often you use it?
grounding: Dana Whitfield: 'I've literally walked to get coffee and come back and it's still loading.'
Dana Whitfield: 'The export plus pivot is faster than waiting on one Pulse query to load.'

questions.primary: If Pulse had a section that showed you the three or four numbers most relevant to your work — no query building required — would that change how often you opened it?
questions.rationale_gap: Dana's core ask was 'make getting a basic pipeline number dead simple.' We want to probe whether pre-built, role-specific dashboards would resolve the adoption barrier broadly.
probes: What would those three or four numbers be for you?
Would you want them to be customizable, or is a sensible default enough?
Is there a risk that pre-built views would be too simplified and you'd still need to dig deeper?
grounding: Dana Whitfield: 'Make getting a basic pipeline number dead simple and fast, that's the whole game.'
Open question: Would pre-built, one-click dashboards for common metrics resolve the adoption barrier for non-technical users?

themes.label: Spreadsheets and source tools become the real source of truth

supporting_quotes.quote: My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse.

themes.label: Data accuracy mismatches and known bugs destroy trust in Pulse numbers

question_bank.research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.
question_bank.personalized_opening: We've been hearing from Finance, Marketing, Support, and Engineering that trust in Pulse numbers is the central blocker — I'd love to understand how that plays out specifically in your team's work.

questions.primary: When Pulse shows a number that doesn't match your source system, what do you do next — and have you ever been able to trace the discrepancy back to a specific cause?
questions.rationale_gap: Four interviewees describe the same loss-of-trust cascade, but no one outside the data engineering team has successfully traced a discrepancy to its root cause; we need to understand whether users even attempt debugging or immediately abandon Pulse.
probes: Did you find out whether it was a definitional difference, a join bug, a timing issue, or something else?
How long did that investigation take, and did it change your use of Pulse afterward?
Has anyone from the data team ever proactively explained a discrepancy to you, or is that always reactive?

Tomás Herrera: 'Last month it showed our paid sessions about 18% lower than GA for the same week. When I can't reconcile it, I just stop believing the dashboard.'
Priya Raman: 'There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed.'

questions.primary: If Pulse showed you — right next to a number — exactly how it was calculated, which source tables it pulled from, and a timestamp of the last refresh, would that change whether you'd present it to a stakeholder?
questions.rationale_gap: Grace, Tomás, and Marcus all cite the inability to explain or trace a number as a hard blocker for executive-facing use; we need to test whether transparency features would meaningfully shift behavior or whether the underlying data quality must be fixed first.
probes: Or is the underlying data accuracy the prerequisite — would lineage transparency only matter after the numbers actually match?
What level of lineage would feel sufficient: a data dictionary, a link to the SQL, a reconciliation report?
Would your CFO / VP accept a number from Pulse if it had a clear audit trail, even if it didn't perfectly match the GL?
grounding: Grace Kim: 'because I can't trace a number in Pulse back to a journal entry, it's a black box, and a black box doesn't reconcile.'
Grace Kim: 'There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at.'
Tomás Herrera: 'if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.'

questions.primary: Are there specific moments in your team's calendar — a weekly review, a monthly close, a board prep — where Pulse data being hours or days stale made it impossible to use?
questions.rationale_gap: We have strong confirmation that staleness is structural, but Grace's month-end lag (2–3 days) may be a distinct pipeline problem on top of the 6-hour sync; mapping staleness to specific workflow moments will sharpen the prioritization case.
probes: How fresh would the data need to be to actually replace your current source-of-truth in that moment — same-day, same-hour?
Is the lag consistent, or does it get worse at certain times like month-end or quarter-end?
Would a visible freshness timestamp help even if the underlying refresh speed didn't change?
grounding: Grace Kim: 'During close it can lag by two or three days. The one window where live analytics would actually help me, it's behind.'
Priya Raman: 'The sync into Pulse only runs every six hours, so the data is stale by design. When the growth team opens a dashboard at 9am, they're looking at numbers from 3am.'
Marcus Lee: 'The ticket data in Pulse is hours stale, it's not real-time. By the time it caught up the fire was already out.'

questions.primary: If the two or three biggest issues were fixed — say, the numbers matched your source system and the data was refreshed in near-real-time — is there something Pulse could do that your current tools can't, or would you still prefer what you're using today?
questions.rationale_gap: Marcus, Tomás, and Grace all expressed conditional willingness to switch; we need to confirm whether cross-team analytics is the genuine differentiator that would drive adoption, or whether teams would still default to their source systems out of habit.
probes: What is the one analysis you currently can't do easily in your source system that Pulse theoretically could handle?
Have you ever seen Pulse produce something genuinely useful that you couldn't replicate elsewhere?
What would it take for your team to standardize on Pulse for a single recurring report?
grounding: Marcus Lee: 'If it's real-time and the counts match, I'd happily switch because Pulse actually slices trends across teams better than Zendesk does.'
Tomás Herrera: 'If I could write raw SQL and the totals actually matched GA and our ad spend, I'd have no reason to rebuild everything in a Google Sheet.'
Grace Kim: 'Make it reconcile to the general ledger, to the dollar, with a clear lineage back to journal entries, and refresh it fast enough to keep up with the close.'

questions.primary: Have you ever heard internally that there's a known data bug in Pulse — specifically a join issue that causes double-counting in some models — and if so, how did you find out?
questions.rationale_gap: Priya confirmed the join bug exists and is the highest-priority fix; we don't know how widely known it is among end users, or whether users are attributing their discrepancies to this bug versus blaming Pulse broadly — which affects how to communicate a fix.
probes: Did knowing about the bug change your behavior — did you stop using certain dashboards, or flag it to others?
Has anyone from product or engineering communicated a timeline for fixing it?
If the bug were fixed and Pulse published a clear note saying 'this model has been corrected,' would that restore your confidence?
grounding: Priya Raman: 'There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed.'

questions.primary: Owen Bradley told us 'a churn signal that's three days old isn't a signal, it's a postmortem' — do you have a specific moment where Pulse data arrived too late to act on, and what did you do instead?

If Pulse showed a clear timestamp of when data was last refreshed, would that change how you use it even before the sync speed improves?
Are there decisions where end-of-day data would be sufficient, versus ones where you truly need intraday?

themes.label: Query builder too complex for power users and non-technical users alike — but for opposite reasons

findings.text: Non-technical users find the query builder overwhelming for simple tasks, while technical users hit a ceiling when they need advanced SQL constructs or API access — the tool satisfies neither end of the spectrum.

supporting_quotes.quote: it's too complex to get a simple pipeline number out of it. I couldn't figure out the query builder at all, all these joins and filters and dimension dropdowns. I just need one number.
The builder is fine for simple cuts, but the second I want a window function or a weird join, I hit a wall. I'm technical, I'd rather just write the query than fight the dropdowns.

Pulse gives you these pre-built views, and the second you want something slightly different, you're stuck. My engineers are perfectly capable of writing SQL. We didn't need hand-holding, we needed access.
I don't want a UI, I want an API and raw query access so I can build what I need. Every time the roadmap is about prettier charts, I think, you're solving the wrong problem for me.

themes.label: Spreadsheets, source tools, and custom-built solutions become the real source of truth

findings.text: Users across Sales Ops, Support, Marketing, Finance, Engineering, and Customer Success bypass Pulse entirely — exporting to spreadsheets, staying in source systems, building their own dashboards, or pulling data manually — which become the team's de facto source of truth.

supporting_quotes.quote: My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse.
We live in Zendesk. I've got a dashboard there for ticket volume, first response time, backlog by queue. That's what we look at in standup every morning. Pulse is kind of off to the side for us.
The sales ops folks ran their standup off Pulse for a week, then went back to a manual export because they couldn't trust intraday numbers.
I export the campaign table, drop in our GA and spend numbers next to it, and reconcile by hand. It's tedious, but at least I know exactly where every figure came from.
the moment something has to be official, I go back to the general ledger. Everything I produce has to reconcile to the penny, and Pulse can't give me that.
I'd rather pull it myself from the source so I know it's right.
About two months ago we gave up and built our own Grafana dashboard off the raw tables. It does exactly what we need and nobody on my team has to fight the tool to get a number out.

themes.label: Slow query performance undermines trust and usability

findings.text: Full-scale queries take so long to load that users abandon Pulse mid-workflow, finding the wait incompatible with time-pressured work; the lag scales with data volume, meaning the heaviest users suffer most.

supporting_quotes.quote: I've literally walked to get coffee and come back and it's still loading.

A toy dataset is fine, but the moment you point it at a quarter of production events, you're staring at a spinner for fifteen, twenty seconds. My team just stopped opening it because waiting felt worse than writing the query ourselves.
The lag scales with how much you actually ask of it, so the people who needed it most got the worst experience.

findings.text: The manual export-plus-pivot workflow, or building a custom dashboard, is faster end-to-end than waiting for a single Pulse query to complete.

supporting_quotes.quote: The export plus pivot is faster than waiting on one Pulse query to load.
If I had an endpoint I trusted and it didn't lag when I loaded anything real, I'd kill our Grafana dashboard tomorrow.

themes.label: Data accuracy mismatches and known bugs destroy trust in Pulse numbers

findings.text: When Pulse figures disagree with source-system numbers, users lose confidence in all Pulse data, stop presenting it to stakeholders, and rebuild figures manually to defend in meetings.

supporting_quotes.quote: Pulse will say we closed 340 tickets yesterday, Zendesk says 312. I have no idea which is right, and if I can't trust the number I'm not going to put it in front of my VP.
The first time the number is wrong, people stop believing all of them. My team already rolls their eyes when I pull up Pulse.
Someone in finance cross-checked revenue against the source system and it didn't match. Once that happens once, they question every number.
Last month it showed our paid sessions about 18% lower than GA for the same week. When I can't reconcile it, I just stop believing the dashboard.

Last quarter a revenue number in Pulse was off from the GL by about forty thousand dollars. I can't put a figure in front of the CFO that doesn't tie out. One mismatch and I lose all trust in the whole tool.
If I'm sitting in front of a customer presenting their own health score, I can't have the number be three days old or have just gotten unblocked that morning.

findings.text: A confirmed join bug causes double-counting in at least one data model; this is a root cause of discrepancies users are noticing and the highest-priority fix according to the data engineer.

supporting_quotes.quote: There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed.
Fix the join bug first. Freshness and access are friction, but the double-count is the thing that quietly kills trust.

findings.text: Users suspect discrepancies stem from definitional differences but feel they shouldn't have to investigate — Pulse offers no metric-definition transparency or data lineage, and the burden of proof falls on the tool.

supporting_quotes.quote: I think Pulse counts merged tickets differently, or maybe it's a timezone thing on the day boundary. But that's the point, right? I shouldn't have to play detective.
if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.

I think it pulls from a different cut of the data with its own logic on refunds and intercompany entries. But because I can't trace a number in Pulse back to a journal entry, it's a black box, and a black box doesn't reconcile.

themes.label: Stale data makes Pulse unusable for operational and time-critical decisions

findings.text: Data is stale by design (6-hour sync cycle, multi-day lags during close, or days-old account health signals), making Pulse structurally unfit for intraday, event-driven, customer-facing, or month-end decisions across multiple teams.

supporting_quotes.quote: The ticket data in Pulse is hours stale, it's not real-time. By the time it caught up the fire was already out.
The sync into Pulse only runs every six hours, so the data is stale by design. When the growth team opens a dashboard at 9am, they're looking at numbers from 3am.
During close it can lag by two or three days. There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at.
A churn signal that's three days old isn't a signal, it's a postmortem. I need to act the morning a usage drop happens, not at the end of the week when the data finally rolls up.
Last month a mid-market account churned and Pulse still showed them green three days after they'd told us they were leaving.

findings.text: Multiple users across teams would actively switch to Pulse if trust, freshness, and access issues were resolved — indicating latent demand, not rejection of the product concept.

supporting_quotes.quote: If it's real-time and the counts match, I'd happily switch because Pulse actually slices trends across teams better than Zendesk does.
If I could write raw SQL and the totals actually matched GA and our ad spend, I'd have no reason to rebuild everything in a Google Sheet.
Make it reconcile to the general ledger, to the dollar, with a clear lineage back to journal entries, and refresh it fast enough to keep up with the close.
Fix the freshness and the access first, those are the dealbreakers.
If I had an endpoint I trusted and it didn't lag when I loaded anything real, I'd kill our Grafana dashboard tomorrow.

themes.label: Broken onboarding and access permissions cause new users and reassigned users to abandon Pulse before first value

findings.text: New analysts encounter greyed-out datasets on joining, and CSMs can't access account-level data for newly assigned accounts until a ticket clears — the multi-day permission delay kills momentum and routes work back to the data team.

supporting_quotes.quote: A new analyst joins, opens Pulse, and half the datasets are greyed out. By the time their permissions clear, they've already given up and asked me to just pull the data for them.
I'm flooded with access-request tickets from people who can't see the tables they need.

I have to file a request with the data team just to see account-level data, and it takes days. So for a brand-new account I'm assigned, I can't even pull their numbers until a ticket clears. That kills any momentum in the first week.

themes.label: Absence of data lineage and freshness indicators forces users to distrust Pulse by default

findings.text: Users cannot see when data was last refreshed or trace a Pulse number back to its source calculation, forcing them to assume the worst and revert to source systems for anything consequential.

supporting_quotes.quote: There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at.
because I can't trace a number in Pulse back to a journal entry, it's a black box, and a black box doesn't reconcile.
With Pulse, if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.
I think Pulse counts merged tickets differently, or maybe it's a timezone thing on the day boundary. But that's the point, right? I shouldn't have to play detective.
Fix the freshness and the access first, those are the dealbreakers.

open_questions: The join bug is the confirmed root cause of at least some discrepancies — which specific data models are affected, and does the finance revenue model (source of Grace's $40K GL mismatch) contain the same bug as the marketing campaign table?
Is there a roadmap or timeline to fix the join bug, and do product/engineering stakeholders understand it as the top trust-blocker across Finance, Marketing, and Support?
Tomás and Hannah both want raw SQL / API access; how many other technical users are hitting the query-builder ceiling, and is a SQL escape-hatch or API endpoint already on the product roadmap?

Hannah's team built a Grafana dashboard off raw tables — how many other engineering or technical teams have similarly routed around Pulse entirely? Is this shadow-infrastructure pattern tracked anywhere?
Hannah cited performance as scaling with query size — is the performance bottleneck in the query engine layer, the data warehouse, or the Pulse application layer? Has engineering investigated this?

Would exposing metric definitions, calculation logic, and data lineage inline (e.g., hover-over tooltips, a data dictionary, or journal-entry traceability for Finance) reduce the 'I can't defend this number' barrier enough to keep users in Pulse?
Is the performance problem widespread across all query types, or concentrated in large cross-dimension, multi-quarter queries?
How many users have abandoned Pulse specifically during the onboarding/permissions phase, and does any team track this drop-off?

What would it take to reduce the sync cadence from 6 hours to near-real-time — and separately, could a data-freshness timestamp be added immediately as a low-effort trust signal even before sync speed improves?

Owen mentioned wanting a proactive per-customer morning digest via email rather than having to log in — is there latent demand for push/alert-style delivery among other operational users (CSMs, Support leads) who need signals without actively querying?
The latent demand signal is now confirmed by five interviewees (Marcus, Tomás, Grace, Owen, Hannah) — is this reflected anywhere in product prioritization discussions?
Grace's month-end lag is 2–3 days and Owen's account health data is days behind — are these the same infrastructure constraint as the 6-hour sync, or separate pipeline delays?

contradictions.description: There is a genuine tension between what different user types need from Pulse's query interface: non-technical users (Dana Whitfield, Marcus Lee, Grace Kim, Owen Bradley) want simpler pre-built dashboards, clear reconciled outputs, and less query-building complexity, while technical users (Tomás Herrera, Hannah Ortiz, and implicitly Priya Raman) want more power — raw SQL access, an API endpoint, window functions, and fewer guardrails. These are mutually exclusive interface philosophies; optimizing for one risks alienating the other. Hannah's team went so far as to abandon Pulse entirely and build a custom Grafana dashboard rather than work within the UI constraints.

question_bank.research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.
question_bank.personalized_opening: We've heard from several technical users that Pulse's query builder is a ceiling rather than a floor — and from at least one engineering team that built their own Grafana dashboard off raw tables rather than keep using Pulse — so I'd love to understand where your team sits on that spectrum.

questions.primary: When Hannah Ortiz's engineering team decided to build a Grafana dashboard off the raw tables instead of using Pulse, they said what they really needed was an API and raw query access — not a better UI. Is that a request you've heard from other technical users, and is there anything on the Pulse roadmap today that addresses it?

probes: Has anyone formally scoped what an API endpoint for Pulse would look like?
Are there security or governance concerns that have blocked raw query access from being built?
Do you know how many teams have routed around Pulse by building their own tooling on top of the raw tables?

Has engineering identified where that bottleneck lives: the query engine, the warehouse, or the application layer?
questions.rationale_gap: Performance problems have now been raised by two users (Dana, Hannah) but neither knew the root cause. We need an engineering or product perspective on whether this is a known, scoped problem or still undiagnosed.
probes: Is there a p95 query latency metric being tracked for Pulse today?
Have any query optimizations or caching layers been attempted?
Does the performance degrade on specific data models or all of them equally?

questions.primary: Hannah's team built a Grafana dashboard off raw tables two months ago and described it as the permanent solution — not a stopgap. Are you aware of other teams that have done something similar, and is there any mechanism to track how many teams have effectively replaced Pulse with custom-built tooling?
questions.rationale_gap: We have confirmed shadow infrastructure from Engineering (Grafana), Support (Zendesk), Marketing (Google Sheets + GA), Finance (GL), and Sales Ops (manual exports). If this is widespread, it suggests a platform-level adoption failure, not individual team quirks.
probes: Do you have visibility into how many teams query the raw data warehouse directly, bypassing Pulse?
Has anyone done a Pulse adoption audit — active users vs. licensed users vs. teams that have churned to alternatives?

Marcus Lee: 'We live in Zendesk.'
Dana Whitfield: 'My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse.'

What's the current status of that fix, and do you know which data models are affected?
questions.rationale_gap: The join bug is the highest-confidence root cause of the trust crisis, but we don't know its fix timeline, which models it affects, or whether product/engineering leadership has prioritized it relative to feature work.
probes: Is the join bug scoped and assigned to an engineer, or still in the backlog?
Does the finance revenue model that produced Grace Kim's $40K GL mismatch share the same bug as the marketing campaign table?
Once fixed, is there a plan to proactively communicate to affected users which historical numbers may have been wrong?

Does that latent-demand signal show up anywhere in how Pulse is currently prioritized, or is the team primarily tracking active usage rather than what would bring churned users back?
questions.rationale_gap: Confirmed latent demand from five interviewees is a strong signal that the product is fixable, but only if the right things get prioritized. We need to know whether product strategy is informed by exit-intent data or only current engagement metrics.
probes: Is there a mechanism for collecting feedback from teams that have stopped using Pulse, or only from active users?
How is the product team currently prioritizing trust/accuracy fixes versus new feature development?
Is Hannah's 'kill our Grafana dashboard tomorrow' conditional offer something the product team has heard before from other engineering teams?

steps.transcript_summary: Raj, a power user, is frustrated that Pulse walls him off from cross-team data and times out on complex queries, and argues he'd adopt it fully if it offered more query power and bigger limits rather than tighter constraints.
