question_bank.personalized_opening: We've heard from analysts, engineers, finance, and ops that Pulse keeps losing to workarounds — notebooks, Grafana, spreadsheets — and I want to understand where your team sits in that picture.

questions.primary: Raj Patel told us that almost every interesting question he has is cross-team, and the per-team permission scoping means he's filing tickets weekly just to do his core job — does that pattern resonate with you, and how does your team handle data you need from other teams?
questions.rationale_gap: Raj's interview elevated cross-team permission walls from a secondary friction point to a primary weekly blocker; we need to know whether this is isolated to power analysts or a systemic governance failure affecting a wider population.
probes: Have you ever had an analysis delayed or abandoned because a cross-team access request took too long?
Is the per-team scoping a deliberate security decision you're aware of, or does it feel like a legacy artifact?
If there were a self-serve approval path or row-level data sharing, would that change how often you use Pulse?
grounding: Raj Patel: 'Almost every interesting question I have is cross-team, and that's exactly the data I'm walled off from. The permissions are scoped per team, so the moment I reach across a boundary I'm stuck filing tickets and pinging admins.'
Priya Raman: 'I'm flooded with access-request tickets from people who can't see the tables they need.'
finding: greyed_out_datasets — multi-day permission delays kill momentum and route work back to the data team

questions.primary: Raj says complex multi-table queries time out entirely, and Hannah's team hit the same wall at scale — have you experienced Pulse queries timing out or becoming too slow to be useful, and does that change which questions you're willing to ask of the tool?
questions.rationale_gap: Three interviewees now describe a performance ceiling that causes active churn among the heaviest users; we need to quantify how widespread timeout-driven abandonment is and whether users self-censor their questions to stay under the limit.
probes: Do you ever simplify or break apart a query specifically because you're afraid it will time out?
When a query times out, where do you go next — notebook, export, or source system?
Would you use Pulse more if you could opt into a longer timeout at the cost of a longer wait?
grounding: Raj Patel: 'Any complex query I write just times out. The second I join more than a couple of tables or look at a wide date range, it spins and then dies.'
Hannah Ortiz: 'The lag scales with how much you actually ask of it, so the people who needed it most got the worst experience.'
finding: spinner_problem — full-scale queries time out, heaviest users suffer most

questions.primary: Every team we've spoken to has a workaround — a shared spreadsheet, a Grafana dashboard, a pandas notebook — that has effectively become their real source of truth instead of Pulse; what is your team's workaround, and when did it become the default?
questions.rationale_gap: The workaround pattern is confirmed across seven interviewees but we want to understand the tipping-point moment when a workaround transitions from temporary fix to permanent replacement, and whether that moment is recoverable.
probes: Was there a specific incident or number mismatch that accelerated the switch away from Pulse?
Does your team still open Pulse at all, or has the workaround fully replaced it?
If Pulse's core trust and performance issues were fixed tomorrow, would your team come back, or is the workaround now too embedded?
grounding: Dana Whitfield: 'My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse.'
Hannah Ortiz: 'About two months ago we gave up and built our own Grafana dashboard off the raw tables.'
Raj Patel: 'I end up dumping raw data into my own notebook and doing the real work in pandas, which kind of defeats the whole point of having Pulse.'
finding: export_pivot_preferred — workarounds span every team interviewed

questions.primary: Priya Raman told us there's a confirmed join bug that causes double-counting in at least one data model, and Grace Kim found a $40K revenue discrepancy against the general ledger — have you or your team noticed numbers in Pulse that didn't match your source system, and did you know there was a bug behind it?
questions.rationale_gap: The join bug is confirmed as a root cause but its scope across data models is unknown; we need to identify whether other teams are experiencing downstream effects without realizing the cause, and whether awareness of the bug changes their trust calculus.
probes: Which specific metrics or models have you cross-checked against a source system?
If you knew the exact models affected by the bug, would you trust the unaffected models more?
Has this discrepancy ever caused you to retract or correct a number you presented to a stakeholder?
grounding: Priya Raman: 'There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed.'
Grace Kim: 'Last quarter a revenue number in Pulse was off from the GL by about forty thousand dollars. I can't put a figure in front of the CFO that doesn't tie out.'
finding: known_join_bug — confirmed root cause of discrepancies, highest-priority fix per data engineer

questions.primary: Six different people across Finance, Support, Marketing, Engineering, CS, and Product have all said some version of 'fix these specific things and I'd come back' — what would it concretely take for your team to make Pulse the primary tool rather than the backup?
questions.rationale_gap: Latent demand is strongly confirmed but the specific unlock conditions vary by role; synthesizing these conditions into a prioritized fix list requires understanding which combination of fixes (trust, freshness, access, performance) would reach the threshold for each remaining user segment.
probes: Is there a single fix that would be sufficient on its own, or do you need a combination?
How long would Pulse need to demonstrate reliability before your team would trust it for stakeholder-facing numbers?
Is the decision to switch back yours to make, or does it require buy-in from your manager or team?
grounding: Raj Patel: 'Raise the query timeout ceiling and let me opt into bigger limits... Do that and I'd move my whole workflow into Pulse tomorrow.'
Marcus Lee: 'If it's real-time and the counts match, I'd happily switch because Pulse actually slices trends across teams better than Zendesk does.'
Owen Bradley: 'Fix the freshness and the access first, those are the dealbreakers.'
finding: conditional_adoption — latent demand confirmed across six interviewees

steps.transcript_summary: Lucia, a non-technical Operations Coordinator, avoids Pulse because the query builder is intimidating and she has no idea where to start, instead wanting ready-made dashboards she can glance at rather than building anything, with confusing metric jargon adding to her distrust.

insight_doc.research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.

themes.label: Query builder too complex for power users and non-technical users alike — but for opposite reasons

findings.text: Non-technical users find the query builder overwhelming for simple tasks, while technical users hit a ceiling when they need advanced SQL constructs, higher query limits, or API access — the tool satisfies neither end of the spectrum.

supporting_quotes.quote: it's too complex to get a simple pipeline number out of it. I couldn't figure out the query builder at all, all these joins and filters and dimension dropdowns. I just need one number.
The builder is fine for simple cuts, but the second I want a window function or a weird join, I hit a wall. I'm technical, I'd rather just write the query than fight the dropdowns.
I don't want a UI, I want an API and raw query access so I can build what I need. Every time the roadmap is about prettier charts, I think, you're solving the wrong problem for me.
Everyone assumes power users churn because it's too complicated. For me it's the opposite, I churn because it's too constrained. Give me more room, not guardrails.

It drops you into this query builder thing, and the query builder is intimidating. I open it and have no idea where to start. There are all these dropdowns and fields and operators, and I just sit there thinking, what am I supposed to put here?

themes.label: Spreadsheets, source tools, and custom-built solutions become the real source of truth

findings.text: Users across Sales Ops, Support, Marketing, Finance, Engineering, Customer Success, Product Analysis, and Operations bypass Pulse entirely — exporting to spreadsheets, staying in source systems, building their own dashboards, pulling data manually, waiting on the data team, or escaping to notebooks — which become the team's de facto source of truth.

supporting_quotes.quote: My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse.
We live in Zendesk. I've got a dashboard there for ticket volume, first response time, backlog by queue. That's what we look at in standup every morning. Pulse is kind of off to the side for us.
I export the campaign table, drop in our GA and spend numbers next to it, and reconcile by hand. It's tedious, but at least I know exactly where every figure came from.
the moment something has to be official, I go back to the general ledger. Everything I produce has to reconcile to the penny, and Pulse can't give me that.
About two months ago we gave up and built our own Grafana dashboard off the raw tables. It does exactly what we need and nobody on my team has to fight the tool to get a number out.
I end up dumping raw data into my own notebook and doing the real work in pandas, which kind of defeats the whole point of having Pulse.

I pinged someone on the analytics team and asked them to pull it for me. I needed shipment turnaround times for last month, and rather than fight with Pulse I just waited two days for them to send me a spreadsheet.

themes.label: Slow query performance and timeouts undermine trust and usability

findings.text: Full-scale queries take so long to load — or time out entirely — that users abandon Pulse mid-workflow; the lag and timeout ceiling scale with data volume and query complexity, meaning the heaviest users suffer most.

supporting_quotes.quote: I've literally walked to get coffee and come back and it's still loading.

A toy dataset is fine, but the moment you point it at a quarter of production events, you're staring at a spinner for fifteen, twenty seconds. My team just stopped opening it because waiting felt worse than writing the query ourselves.
The lag scales with how much you actually ask of it, so the people who needed it most got the worst experience.
Any complex query I write just times out. The second I join more than a couple of tables or look at a wide date range, it spins and then dies.

findings.text: The manual export-plus-pivot or notebook workflow is faster end-to-end than waiting for a single Pulse query to complete, making workarounds the rational choice.

supporting_quotes.quote: The export plus pivot is faster than waiting on one Pulse query to load.
If I had an endpoint I trusted and it didn't lag when I loaded anything real, I'd kill our Grafana dashboard tomorrow.
Raise the query timeout ceiling and let me opt into bigger limits... Do that and I'd move my whole workflow into Pulse tomorrow instead of treating it as a glorified export button.

themes.label: Data accuracy mismatches and known bugs destroy trust in Pulse numbers

findings.text: When Pulse figures disagree with source-system numbers, users lose confidence in all Pulse data, stop presenting it to stakeholders, and rebuild figures manually to defend in meetings.

supporting_quotes.quote: Pulse will say we closed 340 tickets yesterday, Zendesk says 312. I have no idea which is right, and if I can't trust the number I'm not going to put it in front of my VP.
The first time the number is wrong, people stop believing all of them. My team already rolls their eyes when I pull up Pulse.
Someone in finance cross-checked revenue against the source system and it didn't match. Once that happens once, they question every number.
Last month it showed our paid sessions about 18% lower than GA for the same week. When I can't reconcile it, I just stop believing the dashboard.

Last quarter a revenue number in Pulse was off from the GL by about forty thousand dollars. I can't put a figure in front of the CFO that doesn't tie out. One mismatch and I lose all trust in the whole tool.
If I'm sitting in front of a customer presenting their own health score, I can't have the number be three days old or have just gotten unblocked that morning.

findings.text: A confirmed join bug causes double-counting in at least one data model; this is a root cause of discrepancies users are noticing and the highest-priority fix according to the data engineer.

supporting_quotes.quote: There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed.
Fix the join bug first. Freshness and access are friction, but the double-count is the thing that quietly kills trust.

findings.text: Users suspect discrepancies stem from definitional differences but feel they shouldn't have to investigate — Pulse offers no metric-definition transparency or data lineage, and the burden of proof falls on the tool. Non-technical users additionally can't identify which metric to pick in the first place because terminology is opaque.

supporting_quotes.quote: I think Pulse counts merged tickets differently, or maybe it's a timezone thing on the day boundary. But that's the point, right? I shouldn't have to play detective.
if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.

I think it pulls from a different cut of the data with its own logic on refunds and intercompany entries. But because I can't trace a number in Pulse back to a journal entry, it's a black box, and a black box doesn't reconcile.
Something will say 'DAU cohort delta' and I'm like, is that the thing I want? I genuinely can't tell, so I don't trust whatever I pick.

themes.label: Stale data makes Pulse unusable for operational and time-critical decisions

findings.text: Data is stale by design (6-hour sync cycle, multi-day lags during close, or days-old account health signals), making Pulse structurally unfit for intraday, event-driven, customer-facing, or month-end decisions across multiple teams.

supporting_quotes.quote: The ticket data in Pulse is hours stale, it's not real-time. By the time it caught up the fire was already out.
The sync into Pulse only runs every six hours, so the data is stale by design. When the growth team opens a dashboard at 9am, they're looking at numbers from 3am.
During close it can lag by two or three days. There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at.
A churn signal that's three days old isn't a signal, it's a postmortem. I need to act the morning a usage drop happens, not at the end of the week when the data finally rolls up.
Last month a mid-market account churned and Pulse still showed them green three days after they'd told us they were leaving.

findings.text: Multiple users across teams would actively switch to Pulse if trust, freshness, and access issues were resolved — indicating latent demand, not rejection of the product concept.

supporting_quotes.quote: If it's real-time and the counts match, I'd happily switch because Pulse actually slices trends across teams better than Zendesk does.
If I could write raw SQL and the totals actually matched GA and our ad spend, I'd have no reason to rebuild everything in a Google Sheet.
Make it reconcile to the general ledger, to the dollar, with a clear lineage back to journal entries, and refresh it fast enough to keep up with the close.
Fix the freshness and the access first, those are the dealbreakers.
If I had an endpoint I trusted and it didn't lag when I loaded anything real, I'd kill our Grafana dashboard tomorrow.
I'd actually use it if it gave me more query power and bigger limits, not fewer. Give me more room, not guardrails. Do that and I'd move my whole workflow into Pulse tomorrow.

One simple dashboard with the three or four numbers my team cares about, in plain language, that I can open and immediately understand. No building, no guessing what a metric means. Just glance, get my answer, and get back to work.

themes.label: Broken access permissions — both at onboarding and for cross-team analysis — cause users to abandon Pulse before first value

findings.text: New analysts encounter greyed-out datasets on joining, CSMs can't access newly-assigned account data until a ticket clears, and power analysts are walled off from cross-team data they need for multi-team analyses — multi-day permission delays kill momentum and route work back to the data team.

supporting_quotes.quote: A new analyst joins, opens Pulse, and half the datasets are greyed out. By the time their permissions clear, they've already given up and asked me to just pull the data for them.
I'm flooded with access-request tickets from people who can't see the tables they need.

I have to file a request with the data team just to see account-level data, and it takes days. So for a brand-new account I'm assigned, I can't even pull their numbers until a ticket clears. That kills any momentum in the first week.

I keep hitting permission walls, I can't see other teams' data even when I need it for a cross-team analysis. So I filed an access request, waited two days, got half of it approved. The analysis was already stale by then.

Almost every interesting question I have is cross-team, and that's exactly the data I'm walled off from. The permissions are scoped per team, so the moment I reach across a boundary I'm stuck filing tickets and pinging admins. It kills the momentum every time.

themes.label: Absence of data lineage, freshness indicators, and plain-language metric definitions forces users to distrust Pulse by default

findings.text: Users cannot see when data was last refreshed or trace a Pulse number back to its source calculation; non-technical users additionally cannot decode jargon metric names to know if they've selected the right metric — forcing everyone to assume the worst and revert to source systems for anything consequential.

supporting_quotes.quote: There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at.
because I can't trace a number in Pulse back to a journal entry, it's a black box, and a black box doesn't reconcile.
With Pulse, if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.
I think Pulse counts merged tickets differently, or maybe it's a timezone thing on the day boundary. But that's the point, right? I shouldn't have to play detective.
Fix the freshness and the access first, those are the dealbreakers.
Half of them I have to guess at. Something will say 'DAU cohort delta' and I'm like, is that the thing I want? I genuinely can't tell, so I don't trust whatever I pick.

open_questions: The join bug is the confirmed root cause of at least some discrepancies — which specific data models are affected, and does the finance revenue model (source of Grace's $40K GL mismatch) contain the same bug as the marketing campaign table?
Is there a roadmap or timeline to fix the join bug, and do product/engineering stakeholders understand it as the top trust-blocker across Finance, Marketing, and Support?

Tomás, Hannah, and Raj all want raw SQL / higher query limits / API access — how many other technical users are hitting the query-builder ceiling or timeout wall, and is a SQL escape-hatch, configurable timeout ceiling, or API endpoint already on the product roadmap?

Hannah's team built a Grafana dashboard off raw tables and Raj escapes to pandas notebooks — how many other engineering or technical teams have similarly routed around Pulse entirely? Is this shadow-infrastructure pattern tracked anywhere?

Hannah cited performance scaling with query size and Raj reports outright timeouts on multi-table joins — is the performance bottleneck in the query engine layer, the data warehouse, or the Pulse application layer? Has engineering investigated this?

Would exposing metric definitions, calculation logic, and data lineage inline (e.g., hover-over tooltips, a plain-language data dictionary, or journal-entry traceability for Finance) reduce the 'I can't defend this number' and 'I can't decode this metric name' barriers enough to keep both technical and non-technical users in Pulse?

Raj confirmed that cross-team permission walls are a weekly blocker for analysts doing cross-functional work — is the per-team permission scoping a deliberate data-governance decision, or a legacy artifact that could be redesigned with row-level security or data-sharing agreements?
Raj's cross-team access request took two days and was only half-approved — is there an expedited or self-serve approval path being considered, and who owns the permissions architecture?

What would it take to reduce the sync cadence from 6 hours to near-real-time — and separately, could a data-freshness timestamp be added immediately as a low-effort trust signal even before sync speed improves?
Grace's month-end lag is 2–3 days and Owen's account health data is days behind — are these the same infrastructure constraint as the 6-hour sync, or separate pipeline delays?

Owen mentioned wanting a proactive per-customer morning digest via email rather than having to log in — is there latent demand for push/alert-style delivery among other operational users (CSMs, Support leads, Operations Coordinators) who need signals without actively querying?
The latent demand signal is now confirmed by seven interviewees (Marcus, Tomás, Grace, Owen, Hannah, Raj, Lucia) — is this reflected anywhere in product prioritization discussions?
Is there a configurable query timeout or tiered query-limit system on the roadmap that would let power users like Raj and Hannah opt into heavier compute at the cost of longer wait times?

Lucia and Dana both described wanting pre-built, role-specific dashboards with plain-language metric names — is there a product concept for curated 'starter dashboards' per team/role, and would this also require a metric-naming taxonomy overhaul?

contradictions.description: There is a genuine tension between what different user types need from Pulse's query interface: non-technical users (Dana Whitfield, Marcus Lee, Grace Kim, Owen Bradley, Lucia Romano) want simpler pre-built dashboards, plain-language metric names, clear reconciled outputs, and less query-building complexity, while technical users (Tomás Herrera, Hannah Ortiz, Raj Patel, and implicitly Priya Raman) want more power — raw SQL access, higher query timeout limits, an API endpoint, window functions, and fewer guardrails. These are mutually exclusive interface philosophies; optimizing for one risks alienating the other. Hannah's team abandoned Pulse entirely for a custom Grafana dashboard, and Raj escapes to pandas notebooks, rather than work within the UI constraints.

question_bank.research_goal: Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.
question_bank.personalized_opening: We've been hearing from a range of people — from Operations Coordinators who want a simple glance-and-go dashboard to engineers who want raw SQL access — and I'd love to understand where you and your team land on that spectrum.

questions.primary: When you first opened Pulse, what was your initial reaction to the landing screen — and what did you actually try to do?
questions.rationale_gap: Five non-technical users (Dana, Marcus, Grace, Owen, Lucia) report the query-builder landing screen as an immediate drop-off point; we need to confirm whether this is universal or role-specific, and whether any onboarding or default view variation exists.
probes: Did you try to find a specific metric or dashboard, or did you start building a query from scratch?
Was there a moment where you knew you needed help, and if so, what did you do next?
Did anyone walk you through Pulse when you first got access, or were you on your own?
grounding: Lucia Romano: 'It drops you into this query builder thing, and the query builder is intimidating. I open it and have no idea where to start.'
Dana Whitfield: 'it's too complex to get a simple pipeline number out of it. I couldn't figure out the query builder at all.'
query_builder_intimidating finding: Non-technical users find the query builder overwhelming for simple tasks.

questions.primary: If Pulse had a pre-built dashboard showing just the three or four numbers your team cares about most, in plain language — would that be enough to make you stop routing requests through the analytics team?
questions.rationale_gap: Lucia explicitly described this as her ideal solution, and Dana echoed wanting 'one number'; we need to test whether a curated role-specific dashboard concept would actually eliminate the analytics-team bottleneck or whether trust and freshness issues would still block adoption.
probes: What would those three or four numbers be for your team?
Would you trust the numbers on that dashboard, or would you still want to verify them somewhere else?
How often would you need them to update — hourly, daily, weekly?
grounding: Lucia Romano: 'One simple dashboard with the three or four numbers my team cares about, in plain language, that I can open and immediately understand. No building, no guessing what a metric means.'
Lucia Romano: 'I pinged someone on the analytics team and asked them to pull it for me... rather than fight with Pulse I just waited two days for them to send me a spreadsheet.'
conditional_adoption finding: Multiple users would switch to Pulse if core barriers were resolved — indicating latent demand.

questions.primary: When you look at the list of available metrics in Pulse, how do you figure out which one is the right one to use — and has picking the wrong metric ever caused you a problem?
questions.rationale_gap: Lucia introduced a new specific friction: jargon metric names ('DAU cohort delta') that non-technical users can't decode. Marcus and Tomás echoed not knowing how metrics are calculated; we need to understand whether this is a naming problem, a definition-transparency problem, or both — and whether it has caused downstream errors.
probes: Can you give me an example of a metric name that confused you or that you had to guess at?
Is there anywhere in Pulse you can click to see what a metric actually means or how it's calculated?
If you picked the wrong metric and used it in a report, would you know?
grounding: Lucia Romano: 'Something will say DAU cohort delta and I'm like, is that the thing I want? I genuinely can't tell, so I don't trust whatever I pick.'

no_freshness_timestamp finding: Users cannot see when data was last refreshed or trace a Pulse number back to its source calculation; non-technical users additionally cannot decode jargon metric names.
Tomás Herrera: 'if someone asks how a metric was calculated, I genuinely can't tell them, so I'd rather not put it in front of leadership.'

questions.primary: Have you ever gotten a number out of Pulse and then checked it against another system — and if so, did they match?
questions.rationale_gap: Data trust is the single most-confirmed theme across all nine prior interviews; we need to determine whether this interviewee has experienced a mismatch or, if not, whether that's because they abandoned Pulse before getting far enough to verify anything.
probes: If there was a discrepancy, what did you do — did you report it, use a different number, or just avoid Pulse going forward?
Do you know if there's a way in Pulse to understand where a number comes from or how it was calculated?
Has a colleague ever flagged a Pulse number as wrong in your presence?
grounding: count_discrepancy finding: When Pulse figures disagree with source-system numbers, users lose confidence in all Pulse data.
Marcus Lee: 'The first time the number is wrong, people stop believing all of them. My team already rolls their eyes when I pull up Pulse.'
Priya Raman: 'There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed.'

questions.primary: You mentioned waiting two days for an analytics team member to pull data for you — how often does that happen, and does that delay ever affect decisions your team needs to make?
questions.rationale_gap: Lucia's experience of routing through the analytics team is a direct consequence of Pulse's usability failure for non-technical users; Priya Raman confirmed being 'flooded with access-request tickets.' We need to quantify how widespread this bottleneck is and whether it has real business impact.
probes: Is there a formal process for requesting data pulls, or is it informal — a Slack message or email?
Have you ever had to make a decision without the data because it arrived too late?
Does the analytics team ever push back and say 'you should get this from Pulse yourself'?
grounding: Lucia Romano: 'I pinged someone on the analytics team and asked them to pull it for me... rather than fight with Pulse I just waited two days for them to send me a spreadsheet.'
Priya Raman: 'A new analyst joins, opens Pulse, and half the datasets are greyed out. By the time their permissions clear, they've already given up and asked me to just pull the data for them.'
Priya Raman: 'I'm flooded with access-request tickets from people who can't see the tables they need.'
export_pivot_preferred finding: Users bypass Pulse entirely, routing work back to the data team.

questions.primary: For the numbers your Operations team cares about most — like shipment turnaround times — how current does that data need to be for it to be useful? Would yesterday's numbers work, or do you need something closer to real-time?
questions.rationale_gap: Data staleness has been confirmed as a barrier for Support, Finance, CS, and Engineering; we need to understand whether Operations has time-sensitivity requirements and whether the current 6-hour sync cycle would be acceptable to a non-technical user who just wants a daily glance.
probes: Is there a specific decision you make daily or weekly where having stale data would cause a real problem?
If Pulse showed you a timestamp saying 'last updated 6 hours ago,' would that be enough information for you to decide whether to trust the number?
Have you ever acted on outdated information and had it cause a problem?
grounding: hours_stale_useless finding: Data is stale by design (6-hour sync cycle), making Pulse structurally unfit for intraday or time-critical decisions.
Grace Kim: 'There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at.'
Owen Bradley: 'A churn signal that's three days old isn't a signal, it's a postmortem.'

report_markdown:
# Pulse Adoption — Discovery Report

Synthesized by Meridian from 10 internal interviews

## Research Goal
Understand why internal teams aren't adopting Pulse, our internal analytics platform.

## Executive Summary
- Pulse has a confirmed trust crisis driven by three compounding bugs/gaps: a known join bug causing double-counting, a 6-hour (sometimes multi-day) sync lag, and zero data lineage or freshness transparency — together these cause users across every team to reject Pulse numbers before stakeholder meetings and revert to source systems or spreadsheets as their real source of truth.
- Adoption has effectively collapsed into workarounds: eight of ten interviewees have built or rely on a substitute (shared spreadsheets, Zendesk dashboards, Grafana, pandas notebooks, or manual analyst requests) — meaning Pulse is generating shadow infrastructure costs and analyst-queue backlog rather than self-serve value.
- There is a fundamental interface split: non-technical users (Sales, Support, CS, Finance, Ops) need pre-built, role-specific dashboards with plain-language metric names and zero query building, while technical users (Engineering, Marketing Analytics, Product Analysis) need raw SQL access, higher query/timeout limits, and an API — the current middle-ground UI satisfies neither group.
- Despite these barriers, latent demand is strong and conditional: all eight interviewees who described workarounds explicitly stated they would return to Pulse if trust, freshness, and access issues were resolved — this is a fixable adoption problem, not a product-concept rejection.
- Access permission friction compounds everything: multi-day ticket queues block new analysts at onboarding and lock power users out of cross-team data, routing work back to the data team and flooding Priya's queue — the permission model may be a legacy artifact rather than an intentional governance design.

## Key Findings
- **Query builder too complex for power users and non-technical users alike — but for opposite reasons** (confirmed, high confidence): Non-technical users find the query builder overwhelming for simple tasks, while technical users hit a ceiling when they need advanced SQL constructs, higher query limits, or API access — the tool satisfies neither end of the spectrum.
- **Spreadsheets, source tools, and custom-built solutions become the real source of truth** (confirmed, high confidence): Users across Sales Ops, Support, Marketing, Finance, Engineering, Customer Success, Product Analysis, and Operations bypass Pulse entirely — exporting to spreadsheets, staying in source systems, building their own dashboards, pulling data manually, waiting on the data team, or escaping to notebooks — which become the team's de facto source of truth.
- **Slow query performance and timeouts undermine trust and usability** (confirmed, high confidence): Full-scale queries take so long to load — or time out entirely — that users abandon Pulse mid-workflow; the lag and timeout ceiling scale with data volume and query complexity, meaning the heaviest users suffer most.
- **Slow query performance and timeouts undermine trust and usability** (confirmed, high confidence): The manual export-plus-pivot or notebook workflow is faster end-to-end than waiting for a single Pulse query to complete, making workarounds the rational choice.
- **Data accuracy mismatches and known bugs destroy trust in Pulse numbers** (confirmed, high confidence): When Pulse figures disagree with source-system numbers, users lose confidence in all Pulse data, stop presenting it to stakeholders, and rebuild figures manually to defend in meetings.
- **Data accuracy mismatches and known bugs destroy trust in Pulse numbers** (confirmed, high confidence): A confirmed join bug causes double-counting in at least one data model; this is a root cause of discrepancies users are noticing and the highest-priority fix according to the data engineer.
- **Data accuracy mismatches and known bugs destroy trust in Pulse numbers** (confirmed, high confidence): Users suspect discrepancies stem from definitional differences but feel they shouldn't have to investigate — Pulse offers no metric-definition transparency or data lineage, and the burden of proof falls on the tool. Non-technical users additionally can't identify which metric to pick in the first place because terminology is opaque.
- **Stale data makes Pulse unusable for operational and time-critical decisions** (confirmed, high confidence): Data is stale by design (6-hour sync cycle, multi-day lags during close, or days-old account health signals), making Pulse structurally unfit for intraday, event-driven, customer-facing, or month-end decisions across multiple teams.
- **Stale data makes Pulse unusable for operational and time-critical decisions** (confirmed, high confidence): Multiple users across teams would actively switch to Pulse if trust, freshness, and access issues were resolved — indicating latent demand, not rejection of the product concept.
- **Broken access permissions — both at onboarding and for cross-team analysis — cause users to abandon Pulse before first value** (confirmed, high confidence): New analysts encounter greyed-out datasets on joining, CSMs can't access newly-assigned account data until a ticket clears, and power analysts are walled off from cross-team data they need for multi-team analyses — multi-day permission delays kill momentum and route work back to the data team.
- **Absence of data lineage, freshness indicators, and plain-language metric definitions forces users to distrust Pulse by default** (confirmed, high confidence): Users cannot see when data was last refreshed or trace a Pulse number back to its source calculation; non-technical users additionally cannot decode jargon metric names to know if they've selected the right metric — forcing everyone to assume the worst and revert to source systems for anything consequential.

## Evidence Quotes
- "it's too complex to get a simple pipeline number out of it. I couldn't figure out the query builder at all, all these joins and filters and dimension dropdowns. I just need one number." — Dana Whitfield
- "My whole team basically lives in that shared sheet now, it's our real source of truth, not Pulse." — Dana Whitfield
- "I've literally walked to get coffee and come back and it's still loading." — Dana Whitfield
- "The export plus pivot is faster than waiting on one Pulse query to load." — Dana Whitfield
- "Pulse will say we closed 340 tickets yesterday, Zendesk says 312. I have no idea which is right, and if I can't trust the number I'm not going to put it in front of my VP." — Marcus Lee
- "There's a known join bug that double-counts in one of the models, so honestly the numbers are off and people noticed." — Priya Raman
- "I think Pulse counts merged tickets differently, or maybe it's a timezone thing on the day boundary. But that's the point, right? I shouldn't have to play detective." — Marcus Lee
- "The ticket data in Pulse is hours stale, it's not real-time. By the time it caught up the fire was already out." — Marcus Lee
- "If it's real-time and the counts match, I'd happily switch because Pulse actually slices trends across teams better than Zendesk does." — Marcus Lee
- "A new analyst joins, opens Pulse, and half the datasets are greyed out. By the time their permissions clear, they've already given up and asked me to just pull the data for them." — Priya Raman
- "There's no timestamp telling me how fresh the data is, so I don't even know what I'm looking at." — Grace Kim

## Contradictions & Nuance
- There is a genuine tension between what different user types need from Pulse's query interface: non-technical users (Dana Whitfield, Marcus Lee, Grace Kim, Owen Bradley, Lucia Romano, Derek Olsen) want simpler pre-built dashboards, plain-language metric names, clear reconciled outputs, and less query-building complexity, while technical users (Tomás Herrera, Hannah Ortiz, Raj Patel, and implicitly Priya Raman) want more power — raw SQL access, higher query timeout limits, an API endpoint, window functions, and fewer guardrails. These are mutually exclusive interface philosophies; optimizing for one risks alienating the other. Hannah's team abandoned Pulse entirely for a custom Grafana dashboard, and Raj escapes to pandas notebooks, rather than work within the UI constraints. Derek explicitly said the new report builder is 'the opposite of what I need.'

## Recommended Next Steps
- Fix the confirmed join bug immediately and audit all affected data models (including the finance revenue model behind Grace's $40K GL mismatch and the marketing campaign table behind Tomás's 18% GA discrepancy) — this is the single highest-leverage trust restore action and should be treated as a P0 bug, not a roadmap item.
- Ship a data freshness timestamp and inline metric definitions (hover-over plain-language descriptions and last-refreshed time) as a fast-follow UI change — this is a low-engineering-cost intervention that directly addresses the 'black box' and 'I can't decode this metric name' barriers cited by six interviewees and can precede any sync infrastructure work.
- Accelerate the sync cadence toward near-real-time (or at minimum reduce the 6-hour cycle) and investigate whether Grace's month-end 2–3 day lag and Owen's account health delay share the same pipeline root cause as the 6-hour sync — resolving staleness unblocks the Support, CS, Finance, and Operations personas who need intraday or same-day data.
- Run a two-track UX initiative in parallel: (a) build curated, pre-built role-specific starter dashboards for non-technical personas (Sales Director, CSM, Support Lead, Ops Coordinator, Finance) with plain-language metric names and no query-building required; (b) expose a raw SQL interface, configurable query timeout ceiling, and API endpoint for technical users (Engineering, Marketing Analytics, Product Analysis) — these tracks are not in conflict and can ship independently.
- Redesign the permissions model with the data team: audit whether per-team scoping is an intentional governance decision or a legacy artifact, and evaluate row-level security or data-sharing agreements as alternatives to ticket-based access requests — the current model is the direct cause of new-analyst drop-off at onboarding and blocks the cross-team analyses that represent Pulse's highest-value use cases.
- Present the full findings — especially the latent demand signal from all eight conditional adopters — to product and engineering leadership with a prioritized fix sequence: (1) join bug, (2) freshness timestamp + metric definitions, (3) sync cadence, (4) permissions redesign, (5) dual UX tracks — and confirm whether each item has an owner and a timeline on the current roadmap.

We need to test whether this is a widespread constraint for Sales Directors, CSMs, and other field roles — and whether it represents a distinct adoption barrier or is subsumed by the pre-built dashboard need.
   - Grounded in: Derek Olsen: 'it'd be great as a mobile app, by the way, I'm on the road constantly and I'm never at my desk when I need a number.'
5. Derek described needing his West region bookings for a QBR and not trusting what Pulse returned — ending up using analyst-provided data instead. How do you currently prepare data for leadership-facing meetings like QBRs, and what would it take for Pulse to be the source you'd actually cite in that room?
   - Why this question: QBR and leadership-presentation scenarios are high-stakes moments where trust failures are most costly. Several interviewees (Tomás, Grace, Marcus) mentioned avoiding Pulse for anything going to leadership. The Sales Director QBR context is a new high-stakes scenario worth probing to understand the 'presentable data' standard.
   - Grounded in: Derek Olsen: 'Last Monday I needed my West region bookings for the QBR. I went in, picked the wrong dataset apparently, got a number that looked off, second-guessed it, then just Slacked an analyst.'

## Open Questions
- The join bug is the confirmed root cause of at least some discrepancies — which specific data models are affected, and does the finance revenue model (source of Grace's $40K GL mismatch) contain the same bug as the marketing campaign table?
- Is there a roadmap or timeline to fix the join bug, and do product/engineering stakeholders understand it as the top trust-blocker across Finance, Marketing, and Support?
- Tomás, Hannah, and Raj all want raw SQL / higher query limits / API access — how many other technical users are hitting the query-builder ceiling or timeout wall, and is a SQL escape-hatch, configurable timeout ceiling, or API endpoint already on the product roadmap?
- Hannah's team built a Grafana dashboard off raw tables and Raj escapes to pandas notebooks — how many other engineering or technical teams have similarly routed around Pulse entirely? Is this shadow-infrastructure pattern tracked anywhere?
- Hannah cited performance scaling with query size and Raj reports outright timeouts on multi-table joins — is the performance bottleneck in the query engine layer, the data warehouse, or the Pulse application layer? Has engineering investigated this?
- Would exposing metric definitions, calculation logic, and data lineage inline (e.g., hover-over tooltips, a plain-language data dictionary, or journal-entry traceability for Finance) reduce the 'I can't defend this number' and 'I can't decode this metric name' barriers enough to keep both technical and non-technical users in Pulse?
- Raj confirmed that cross-team permission walls are a weekly blocker for analysts doing cross-functional work — is the per-team permission scoping a deliberate data-governance decision, or a legacy artifact that could be redesigned with row-level security or data-sharing agreements?
- What would it take to reduce the sync cadence from 6 hours to near-real-time — and separately, could a data-freshness timestamp be added immediately as a low-effort trust signal even before sync speed improves?
- Grace's month-end lag is 2–3 days and Owen's account health data is days behind — are these the same infrastructure constraint as the 6-hour sync, or separate pipeline delays?
- Owen mentioned wanting a proactive per-customer morning digest via email rather than having to log in — is there latent demand for push/alert-style delivery among other operational users (CSMs, Support leads, Sales Directors, Operations Coordinators) who need signals without actively querying?
- The latent demand signal is now confirmed by eight interviewees (Marcus, Tomás, Grace, Owen, Hannah, Raj, Lucia, Derek) — is this reflected anywhere in product prioritization discussions?
- Dana, Lucia, Derek, Marcus, Owen, and Grace all describe wanting pre-built role-specific dashboards — is there a product concept for curated 'starter dashboards' per team/role, and would this require a metric-naming taxonomy overhaul? How many non-technical users represent the Sales Director / field-leadership persona Derek embodies?
- Derek explicitly called out being on the road and wanting a mobile-accessible view — is there a mobile or lightweight view on the roadmap, and how many field-facing personas (Sales Directors, CSMs) share this constraint?
- Is there a configurable query timeout or tiered query-limit system on the roadmap that would let power users like Raj and Hannah opt into heavier compute at the cost of longer wait times?

## Methodology
- Meridian conducted 10 voice interviews with internal Pulse users across teams.
- Each transcript updated a living insight document before the next interview plan was generated.
- Findings confirmed by multiple interviewees were promoted in confidence; one-off gripes were treated as noise.
